claimText: The 2025 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for discoveries concerning peripheral immune tolerance.
evidenceExcerpt: Mary E. Brunkow, Fred Ramsdell and Shimon Sakaguchi have been awarded Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2025 for their discoveries concerning peripheral immune tolerance.
examUse: Statement-based question on recent Nobel prizes in science.

claimText: Central immune tolerance occurs in the thymus, where T cells are produced and trained.
evidenceExcerpt: Central Tolerance happens in the thymus, a small gland in the lymphatic system that produces and trains T cells.
examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question distinguishing central vs. peripheral tolerance.

claimText: Peripheral immune tolerance relies on Regulatory T cells (Treg cells) to identify and stop self-reactive T cells from attacking the body's own tissues.
evidenceExcerpt: Peripheral Tolerance uses Regulatory T cells (Treg cells) to identify self-reactive T cells and stop them from attacking our own tissues.
examUse: Statement-based question on the function of Treg cells or peripheral tolerance.

claimText: The FOXP3 gene controls the development and function of regulatory T cells (Treg cells).
evidenceExcerpt: Mary Brunkow and Fred Ramsdell discovered the FOXP3 gene, which controls the development and function of regulatory T cells.
examUse: Statement-based question on genetics of the immune system.

claimText: A mutation in the FOXP3 gene prevents the proper formation of regulatory T cells, leading to a rare autoimmune disease called IPEX.
evidenceExcerpt: If there is a mutation in the FOXP3 gene, regulatory T cells do not form properly, causing a rare autoimmune disease called IPEX.
examUse: Correct-pair question (Disease vs. Gene mutation) or statement-based question on autoimmune diseases.
